content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 24, 1993


David B. Cosgrove
Rutan and Tucker
Bank of the West, Suite 1400
611 Anton Blvd.
Costa Mesa, CA 92626-1998

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-93-238

Dear Mr. Cosgrove:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
ing the responsibilities of the members of the Palm Springs'
Visitors and Promotion Board, pursuant to conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 You are
specifically seeking informal assistance with respect to your
question.2

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Does the "public generally" exception apply to permit members
of the Palm Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board to participate
in decisions that may have a material financial effect on the
member's economic interests?


1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-93-238
Page 2


CONCLUSION

Under the "public generally" exception, members of the Palm
Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board may only participate in the
decisions of the board that materially affect their economic
interests if the decisions will affect a significant segment of
the industry they represent in substantially the same manner.
However, nonindustry members, such as the city council
representatives and the public members, may only participate in
decisions that materially affect their economic interests if the
decisions will affect a significant segment of the population of
Palm Springs in substantially the same manner.

FACTS

In March of 1989, the Palm Springs City Council created the
Palm Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board (the "board"). The
board is composed of eleven members, five of whom are required by the
creating ordinance to be hotel owners or managers in the
jurisdiction, and three members representing the retail and
restaurant sector of the city. Additionally, there are two city
councilmembers and two members selected to represent the public at
large.

The board was created to oversee and advise on the hiring of
an officer to oversee advertising and promotion for the city; the
promotion, advertising, publicizing, and marketing activities of
the City of Palm Springs; to evaluate marketing efforts of the
city; to serve as a liaison between the hospitality industry
valley-wide and the Palm Springs effort; and to make
recommendations on the annual budget to be used for the city's
visitors promotion activity.

You stated that since its inception, the board has instituted
a central clearinghouse for information on hotels and a hotel
referral service, and a discount coupon program which circulated
discount coupons to retail businesses and local merchants in the
city.

ANALYSIS

Conflicts of Interest

The Act was adopted by the voters of California by initiative
in 1974. The purpose of the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or
appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in mak-
ing, or otherwise using the official's position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial

Our File No. I-93-238
Page 3


interest. A "public official" is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.
This definition would appear to include members of the Palm
Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board.

Section 87103 provides:

     An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

* * *

     For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
directly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

You stated in your letter requesting advice, that the members
of the Palm Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board were appointed
by virtue of having interests in specific businesses, trades, and
professions in the jurisdiction. These business interests are

Our File No. I-93-238
Page 4


considered economic interests pursuant to Section 87103. Thus,
the members are prohibited from participating in decisions that
will have a foreseeable3 and material financial effect on the
interests.

However, we note that the councilmembers and the public
members are not appointed by virtue of their economic interests,
or to represent any specific industry, trade, or profession in the
jurisdiction. Thus, we cannot analyze whether these members have
economic interests that will be affected by the board's decisions.
However, if these members do have economic interests that will be
affected, they are prohibited from participating in decisions that
will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the
interests.

The Commission has adopted guidelines to determine whether a
financial effect on an economic interest is material, depending on
the specific circumstances of the decision. The applicable
standard differs depending on whether an economic interest is
directly or indirectly affected by a decision.

Regulation 18702.1 provides that where a business entity in
which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in the decision before the public official's agency, the
effect of a decision is deemed to be material. A business is
directly involved in a decision before the board when the business
entity, or its agent:

     (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

     (2) Is a named party in, or the subject of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency;

     (3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

Regulation 18702.1(b).


3 Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Our File No. I-93-238
Page 5


     However, an economic interest may also result in
disqualification with respect to a decision if the economic
interest will be indirectly materially affected.

     Whether the indirect effect on a business entity is material
depends on the financial size of the business entity. For
example, Regulation 18702.2(g) provides that for a relatively
small business entity, the indirect effect of a decision is
material where:

     (1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

     (2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

     (3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

     Consequently, if subdivision (g) of Regulation 18702.2 were
the appropriate standard, and the board's decision concerned a
program that will affect the gross revenues of a member's business
or the member's employer by $10,000 or more for a fiscal year, or
the value of assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more, the member
may not participate. Moreover, if the decision will result in the
member's business incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $2,500 or more, the member may not participate.

"Public Generally" Exception

     However, public officials with financial interests that will
be materially affected by a decision may participate in the
decision if the effect on their interests is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. This exception is
factually based and takes several different forms depending on the
specific facts involved.

     The most common application of the "public generally"
exception is to decisions that affect the official's interests in
substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant
segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.) The "public" consists
of the entire jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re Owen
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) This is so because all of the residents of
the jurisdiction are constituents of the official.

     Thus, the members of the board could participate in decisions
where the effect on the member's economic interest is
substantially the same as the effect on a significant segment of
the population of the City of Palm Springs.

Our File No. I-93-238
Page 6


Regulation 18703(c) and (d) also provide that an industry,
trade, or profession may constitute the public generally if the
statute, ordinance, or other provision of law which creates or
authorizes the creation of the agency or office contains the
following finding and declaration:

     The Legislature [or other authority] declares
     that the individual[s] appointed to the office of
     is [are] intended to represent and further the
     interest of the [specified industry, trade or
     profession], and that such representation and
     furtherance will ultimately serve the public
     interest. Accordingly, the Legislature [or other
     authority] finds that for purposes of persons who
     hold such office the [specified industry, trade or
     profession] is tantamount to and constitutes the
     public generally within the meaning of Section
     87103 of the Government Code.

     In the alternative, in the absence of an express finding and
declaration, an industry, trade, or profession may still
constitute the public generally if it is implicit, taking into
account the language of the creating statute, ordinance, or other
provision of law, the nature and purposes of the program, any
applicable legislative history, and any other relevant
circumstance that the creating authority intends that for purposes
of persons who hold such office, the specified industry, trade or
profession is tantamount to and constitutes the public generally
within the meaning of Section 87103.

     In light of this exception, as you noted, in 1981, the
Commission considered application of this exception to landlords
owning four or more rental units who were appointed to a rent
stabilization board. (In re Overstreet (1981) 6 FPPC Ops. 12.)
The Commission found that implicit in the formation of the board
was the finding in Regulation 18703(c) that the rental property
industry was a significant segment of the population, and that the
landlord/commissioners were appointed to further the interests of
owners of rental property. Consequently, so long as a decision
affected the entire rental property industry (four or more units),
it was presumed to be a significant segment of the population and
the landlord/commissioners could participate. (In re Overstreet,
supra, at p. 21.)

     Similarly, while tenant/commissioners were not appointed to
represent an industry, trade, or profession and therefore could
not invoke the exception in Regulation 18703(c) and (d), the
Commission did find in the Overstreet Opinion that tenants were a
significant segment of the general public as a whole. The
Commission advised:

     Consequently, Ms. Sage can participate in
     decisions of the Rent Stabilization Board which

Our File No. I-93-238
Page 7


will have a material financial effect on her
interest in the property she rents, if those
decisions will affect her interests in
substantially the same manner as they will affect
tenants in general in Berkeley. Thus, in carrying
out her duties to advise the Rent Stabilization
Board concerning litigation, if the litigation
concerns the constitutionality of Measure D or an
interpretation of it which will apply to all
tenants in Berkeley, Ms. Sage is not required to
disqualify herself from participating in the
Board's decisions concerning the litigation by
making substantive recommendations to them. On the
other hand, if the effect of litigation concerning
the Board on her interest in real property will be
distinguishable from the litigation's effects on
tenants in general, she should disqualify herself.

Emphasis added.

According to your facts, the Palm Springs' Visitors and
Promotion Board was created to promote and enhance the tourist
industry in Palm Springs, and thereby economically benefit the
city as a whole. In furtherance of this goal, the city council
determined that certain businesses should be represented on the
board. Thus, the Municipal Code provides that the board will
consist of the following:

1. An owner or manager of a hotel with over 250 rooms.

2. An owner or manager of a hotel with 150 to 249 rooms.

3. An owner or manager of a hotel with 51 to 149 rooms.

4. An owner or manager of a hotel with 1 to 50 rooms.

5. Three members of the retail and restaurant segments of
the business community.

6. Two members from the public at-large.

7. Two members of the city Council.

Implicit in these appointments was the belief that these
industries (the hotel, restaurant, and retail industries) were
most closely related to the tourism industry in the city, and that
by benefiting these businesses, the city would benefit the
business climate of the city as a whole. Thus, it appears the
exemption in Regulation 18703(d) would apply to the members of the
board.

However, please note, even where an appointed official falls
within the scope of the exception in Regulation 18703(c) or (d),
we have consistently cautioned that if a decision will have an

Our File No. I-93-238
Page 8


unusual effect on the economic interests of the member,
disqualification may still be required. The Commission advised in
the Overstreet Opinion: "Therefore... Mr. Overstreet is not
disqualified from participating in any decision. If the decision
will have a similar effect throughout the Berkeley rental property
industry." (In re Overstreet, supra, at p. 21, emphasis added.)

Consequently, the members may only participate in the
decisions of the board if the decisions will affect a significant
segment of the industry as a whole in substantially the same
manner.

However, the exception would not be applicable to nonindustry
members, such as the city council representatives and the public
members who are appointed to represent the position as a
whole. (Beautrow Advice Letter, No. I-89-042.) If a decision was
to materially affect their economic interest, they would only be
able to participate if the decisions will affect a significant
segment of Palm Springs in substantially the same manner. Of
course, as stated above, due to the nature of the board, it would
be unlikely that the economic interests of these members will be
materially affected by decisions of the board.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.4


Sincerely,


Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
    Counsel, Legal Division


4    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

we have consistently cautioned that if a decision will have an


Our Page8
unusual effect on the economic interests of the member,
disqualification may still be required.
The Commission advised in
the Overstreet Opinion:
“Therefore...
Mr. Overstreet is not
disqualified from participating in any decision..
. if the decision
will have a similar effect throughout the Berkeley rental property
industry.”
(In re Overstreet, supra, at p.
21,
emphasis added.)
Consequently, the members may only participate in the
decisions of the board if the decisions will affect a significant
segment of the industry as a whole in substantially the same
manner.
However, the exception would not be applicable to nonindustry
members,
such as the city council representatives and the public
members who are appointed to represent the jurisdiction as a
whole.
(Beautrow Advice Letter,
No.
1-89—042.)
If a decision was
to materially affect their economic interest, they would only be
able to participate, if the decisions will affect a significant
segment of Palm Springs in substantially the same manner.
Of
course,
as stated above,
due to the nature of the board, it would
be unlikely that the economic interests of these members will be
materially affected by decisions of the board.

CONCLUSION: Under the “public generally” exception, members of the Palm
Springs’ Visitors and Promotion Board may only participate in the
decisions of the board that materially affect their economic
interests if the decisions will affect a significant segment of

Palm Springs in substantially the same manner.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission June 24, 1993 David B. Cosgrove Rutan and Tucker Bank of the West, Suite 1400 611 Anton Blvd. Costa Mesa, CA 92626-1998 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-93-238 Dear Mr. Cosgrove: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard- ing the responsibilities of the members of the Palm Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board, pursuant to conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 You are specifically seeking informal assistance with respect to your question.2 Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTION Does the "public generally" exception apply to permit members of the Palm Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board to participate in decisions that may have a material financial effect on the member's economic interests? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. I-93-238 Page 2 CONCLUSION Under the "public generally" exception, members of the Palm Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board may only participate in the decisions of the board that materially affect their economic interests if the decisions will affect a significant segment of the industry they represent in substantially the same manner. However, nonindustry members, such as the city council representatives and the public members, may only participate in decisions that materially affect their economic interests if the decisions will affect a significant segment of the population of Palm Springs in substantially the same manner. FACTS In March of 1989, the Palm Springs City Council created the Palm Springs' Visitors and Promotion Board (the "board"). The board is composed of eleven members, five of whom are required by the creating ordinance to be hotel owners or managers in the jurisdiction, and three members representing the retail and restaurant sector of the city. Additionally, there are two city councilmembers and two members selected to represent the public at large. The board was created to oversee and advise on the hiring of an officer to oversee advertising and promotion for the city; the promotion, advertising, publicizing, and marketing activities of the City of Palm Springs; to evaluate marketing efforts of the city; to serve as a liaison between the hospitality industry valley-wide and the Palm Springs effort; and to make recommendations on the annual budget to be used for the city's visitors promotion
